data.title: Lawyers for US citizen shot by Border Patrol agent accuse Trump administration of spreading lies
data.description:
The lawyers for a Chicago woman who was shot five times by a U.S. Border Patrol agent accused the Trump administration on Wednesday of spreading lies about the shooting and of mishandling the investigation.

Marimar Martinez, a U.S. citizen, was in her car in October when a Border Patrol agent shot her. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) pressed charges, accusing her attempting to use her car to ram into the agents at the scene.

But video footage released by her attorneys — which showed an officer steering his vehicle into Martinez’s car — led to the case’s dismissal.

“This is a time where we just cannot trust the words of our federal officials,” attorney Christopher Parente told reporters.

Her lawyers accused DHS of lying after the department called her a “domestic terrorist” with a history of doxing federal officials.

The evidence released also included a hand-drawn diagram showing that federal agents “boxed in” Martinez’s car and included other vehicles that, according to Parente, “don’t exist.”

“Most people, after their criminal indictment has been dismissed, would stop fighting, but to have your own government call you something as hurtful and as harmful as a domestic terrorist when you know you’re not is just unacceptable,” he said.

The agent who shot Martinez five times, Charles Exum, was not wearing a body camera, NBC News reported. Other officials with body cameras show Exum steering his vehicle before colliding with Martinez’s car. He leaves the vehicle and five shots can be heard.

The U.S. Attorney’s office also released emails and texts, along with the videos, on Tuesday night. Text exchanges to and from Exum show that he received “big time” support after the shooting, the outlet reported. He texted at one point that this support also came from DHS Secretary Kristi Noem and former Border Patrol Commander-at-Large Greg Bovino.

The Hill reached out Customs and Border Protection (CBP) for comment.

The agency told The Associated Press that Exum was placed on administrative leave and that use-of-force incidents “are thoroughly investigated.”

DHS Assistant Secretary Tricia McLaughlin previously said in a statement that Martinez allegedly carried a “semi-automatic weapon” and claimed that she doxed federal officers.

Martinez has a valid concealed-carry license and carries a gun in her purse, of which her attorneys released a photo. They said the gun was holstered in her purse throughout the incident with Border Patrol agents.

Noem later accused U.S. citizens Renee Good and Alex Pretti of domestic terrorism after they were killed in separate shootings in Minneapolis during federal immigration operations last month. The two deaths led to public outcry, protests, growing tensions between Minnesota officials and the Trump administration, and Democratic lawmakers calling for reforms of DHS agencies, including Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE).

On Thursday, White House border czar Tom Homan said the operation in Minnesota will conclude, with a “significant drawdown” this week and through next week.

The Associated Press contributed.

data.title: Former Army colonel sentenced to 2 years for sharing classified war plans with woman he met online
data.description:
A retired United States Army colonel has been sentenced to two years in federal prison for sending a woman he met online classified information about U.S. war plans, federal authorities announced on Wednesday. 

Kevin Charles Luke, 62, pleaded guilty in October 2025, admitting to abusing a position of public trust as part of his plea agreement.

Prosecutors accused Luke of sharing classified information in October 2024, while a civilian employee at United States Central Command in Tampa, Florida.

“sent to my boss earlier, gives you a peek at what I do for a living,” Luke wrote to the woman in a text message from his personal phone, followed by a photo of a computer screen that displayed an email he had sent using his government email address.

That email revealed targets of a planned U.S. military operation, the future date of the operation, the means of executing the operation and the goal of the operation, according to prosecutors.

“The unauthorized release of the information contained within the photograph could be expected to cause serious damage to the national security of the United States,” the U.S. Attorney’s Office for the Middle District of Florida said in a statement.

Luke served in active duty and reserve roles in the Army from 1981 until his retirement with the rank of Colonel in 2018.

After retiring, he worked as a contractor and a civilian Army employee. He held a security clearance that authorized him to access classified information related to the national defense as a civilian employee and throughout that time, according to the U.S. Attorney’s office.

The sentence was handed down by U.S. District Judge James Moody and announced by U.S. Attorney Gregory Kehoe.

data.title: Democrats launch probe into organization leading White House efforts to celebrate nation's 250th birthday
data.description:
A handful of Democratic senators on Wednesday launched a probe into a private group that is co-leading America’s 250th anniversary celebration. 

The Freedom 250 group is working alongside the America250 commission which was congressionally authorized more than a decade ago to plan a nonpartisan, civic-focused commemoration of the 250th anniversary of the nation’s founding.

“Recent reporting indicates that Freedom 250 is soliciting large private donations while offering donors exclusive access and other benefits tied to the President,” Democratic Sens. Chris Van Hollen (Md.), Adam Schiff (Calif.), Elizabeth Warren (Mass.), Richard Blumenthal (Conn.), Cory Booker (N.J.), Dick Durbin (Ill.) and Gary Peters (Mich.) wrote in their letter to Susie Wiles, the White House chief of staff.

“These reports raise serious questions about transparency, potential conflicts of interest, and whether access to the Administration’s official or quasi-official activities is being conditioned on or provided in exchange for financial contributions,” they added.

Senators say Meredith O’Rourke is spearheading donor relations for the Freedom 250 group and the White House ballroom renovation project, which is also being funded by private benefactors. 

In both fundraising efforts, those who give $1 million or more have been promised photo-ops with President Trump, invitations to private events sponsored by the White House and in some cases name recognition in spaces on the campus grounds.

The group of Democratic senators said the actions could cross into the territory of federal bribery, conflict of interest or violation of ethics statutes.

“These concerns are heightened where events are sponsored, hosted, or facilitated by the White House or federal agencies, yet appear to serve President Trump’s political agenda and allies while presenting opportunities for donor recognition that resemble political fundraising activity,” the lawmakers wrote.

“Government-sponsored civic commemorations should not serve as platforms for political messaging or partisan activity, nor should they create opportunities for donors to exert influence with federal decision-makers under the guise of patriotic celebration. Moreover, the use of a private entity to raise funds for activities closely associated with the presidency risks circumventing transparency, disclosure, and guardrails that ordinarily apply to campaign fundraising or official government operations,” they added.

In response to those concerns, White House spokesperson Davis Ingle slammed Schiff, who signed the Wednesday letter, as someone who “hates” President Trump.

“President Trump is ensuring that America gets the spectacular birthday it deserves. The celebration of America’s 250th anniversary is going to display great patriotism in our Nation’s Capital and throughout the country,” Ingle wrote in a statement to The Hill.

“It’s a complete disgrace that Pencil-Neck Adam Schiff is willing to trash the United States of America during its semiquincentennial celebration simply because he hates President Trump. The great people of California deserve better,” he added.

Amid fundraising concerns, the president has touted the America 250 celebration as the “most spectacular birthday party the world has ever seen.” 

Plans include illuminating the Washington Monument, the construction of an arch, a fair on the National Mall and the development of a National Garden of American Heroes backed with $40 million from Congress.

There are also plans to have a national sporting competition for high school athletes in the proposed first-ever “Patriot Games,” a four-day athletic event for top high school athletes involving one young man and one young woman from each state and territory. Events will culminate with a UFC fight on the White House South Lawn.

“2026 will be a celebration of America like no other, honoring our nation in all of its glory. To help carry out these exciting plans, we have created a new public-private partnership. It’s called Freedom 250,” Trump said in a video posted the social platform X.

“Working with states, companies, and organizations across the country, we will renew the patriotism, pride, and pioneering spirit of America, and lay the groundwork for the next 250 years of independence and freedom. This will be a time like you’ve never had in your lives, America 250,” he added.

data.title: CNN's Collins: Leavitt 'to her credit' pushed Saudis on attending event
data.description:
CNN anchor Kaitlan Collins credited White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt for allowing her to cover an event last year involving President Trump and Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman despite pushback from Saudi officials.

During an interview published Wednesday with Heather McMahan on the “Absolutely Not” podcast, Collins recalled when the White House press corps accompanied Trump on a visit to the Kingdom last May. She said that when she shouted a question at the president while he was with Prince Mohammed, the Saudi Royal Guard “kind of freaked out, because I had dared to ask a question and they’re not used to that there because they don’t have a free press.”

Collins, who is also CNN’s chief White House correspondent, said that Saudi officials told her that she couldn’t cover the next event, which was set to start shortly. Younger White House staffers then went to Leavitt, who Collins said diffused the situation.

“To her credit, she said, ‘No, Kaitlan’s coming in with the rest of the U.S. press.’ And we went in, and it didn’t become this huge issue,” Collins noted.

The CNN host later said the move was “important in that moment, especially when you’re the U.S. contingent abroad, and we don’t do things like they do in Saudi Arabia.” 

Collins has clashed with Trump and his administration throughout his second term. Earlier this month, he insulted her in the Oval Office after she asked him about Elon Musk and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick’s presence in the Jeffrey Epstein files.

“I think it’s time now for the country to, maybe, get on to something else,” the president said on the Epstein files. “Like health care, or something that people care about.”

Collins cut him off, asking, “But what would you say to people who feel like they haven’t gotten justice, Mr. President? What would you say to the survivors who feel like they haven’t gotten justice?”

“You are the worst reporter,” Trump replied. “CNN has no ratings because of people like you. You know she’s a young woman — I don’t think I’ve ever seen you smile. I’ve known you for 10 years. I don’t think I’ve ever seen a smile on your face. You know why you’re not smiling? Because you know you’re not telling the truth.”

CNN anchor Jake Tapper defended his colleague at the time, writing on social media that Collins “is a great reporter & person.”

data.title: Fetterman only Senate Democrat to vote to keep Homeland Security funded
data.description:
Sen. John Fetterman (Pa.) was the only Democratic senator to vote to advance a full-year funding package for the Department of Homeland Security, sticking with his long-held position of opposing government shutdowns for any reason.

Fetterman, like many Democratic and Republican colleagues, has expressed sympathy and condolences for the families of the two protesters shot and killed in Minneapolis but he has declared repeatedly that he would never vote to shut the government down.

On Thursday he voted with Republicans to advance full-year Homeland Security funding, which included funding for Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers to wear body cameras and for de-escalation training for federal immigration officers.

Other Democrats, including centrist Sens. Catherine Cortez Masto (D-Nev.) and Angus King (Maine), an independent who caucuses with Democrats, voted against the Homeland Security funding bill.

Cortez Masto and King voted with Fetterman multiple times for a House-passed funding measure to end the 43-day government shutdown in the fall.

But Cortez Masto said she would oppose even a temporary funding extension for the Homeland Security Department because of what she called a lack of progress on talks to reform ICE and Customs and Border Protection.

“The Republicans have to work with us, and they haven’t even come to the table on addressing our concerns,” she said after voting against advancing the funding bill.

Fetterman pushed back on fellow Democrats on another high-profile issue, recently, when he defended calls to require Americans to show identification to vote.

“It’s not a radical idea for regular Americans to show your ID to vote, and absolutely those things are not Jim Crow or anything,” Fetterman said over the weekend. “Of course, that’s part of an awful, awful legacy of our nation.”

data.title: AI safety researcher quits Anthropic, warning 'world is in peril'
data.description:
A pair of researchers resigned from Anthropic and OpenAI this week, with one warning that the “world is in peril” from a multitude of crises.

Mrinank Sharma announced his resignation from Anthropic in an open letter to his colleagues on Monday. Sharma, who has served on the company’s technical staff since 2023, first noted that he “achieved what I wanted to here” and is “especially proud of my recent efforts to help us live our values via internal transparency mechanisms; and also my final project on understanding how AI assistants could make us less human or distort our humanity.”

“Nevertheless, it is clear to me that the time has come to move on,” he wrote later in the letter. “I continuously find myself reckoning with our situation. The world is in peril. And not just from AI, or bioweapons, but from a whole series of interconnected crises unfolding in this very moment.

“[Throughout] my time here, I’ve repeatedly seen how hard it is truly let our values govern actions,” Sharma added. “I’ve seen this within myself, within the organization, where we constantly face pressures to set aside what matters most, and throughout broader society too.”

Two days later, Zoë Hitzig wrote in an op-ed for The New York Times that she was resigning from OpenAI over the company deciding to test ads on its ChatGPT platform. Hitzig, a research scientist with the company since 2024, said that while she does not believe advertisements are “immoral and unethical,” she still has “deep reservations about OpenAI’s strategy.”

“Users are interacting with an adaptive, conversational voice to which they have revealed their most private thoughts,” the researcher wrote. “People tell chatbots about their medical fears, their relationship problems, their beliefs about God and the afterlife. 

“Advertising built on that archive creates a potential for manipulating users in ways we don’t have the tools to understand, let alone prevent.”

A spokesperson for OpenAI directed The Hill to its advertising principles, which state that ads do not influence the answers ChatGPT provides, as well as CEO of Applications Fidji Simo telling journalist Alex Heath earlier this week that “ads are always going to be very clearly separate and delineated from the content, so you know exactly what you are getting.”

The Hill has also reached out to Anthropic regarding Sharma’s concerns.

Claude, Anthropic’s chatbot, released multiple ads earlier this month mocking OpenAI for its decision to display ads on ChatGPT. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman said at the Harvard Business School in 2024 that he is not “totally against” ads on chatbots but the idea is “uniquely unsettling.”

Altman fired back at the ads — which aired during the Super Bowl — writing on social media that they are “clearly dishonest” and “deceptive.”

data.title: Lawmakers express bipartisan outrage as DOJ accused of 'spying on members' Epstein searches
data.description:
Lawmakers on both sides of the aisle are accusing the Justice Department of spying on them as they reviewed the unredacted Epstein files on DOJ computers, tracking the documents they examined.

Attorney General Pam Bondi was photographed during a congressional appearance before the House Judiciary Committee Wednesday flipping to a document titled “Jayapal Pramila Search History,” seemingly reviewing the activity of the Washington Democrat who tangled with Bondi during the hearing.

Bondi had a binder at the ready of stats and crime summaries ready to hurl at lawmakers during the contentious hearing, but members are unnerved that DOJ apparently tracked their searches to have at the ready.

“It is totally inappropriate and against the separations of powers for the DOJ to surveil us as we search the Epstein files. Bondi showed up today with a burn book that held a printed search history of exactly what emails I searched. That is outrageous and I intend to pursue this and stop this spying on members,” Rep. Pramila Jayapal (D-Wash.) wrote on X after seeing the photo.

Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) also expressed alarm, calling any effort to monitor lawmakers “inappropriate.”

“I think members should obviously have the right to peruse those at their own speed and with their own discretion. And I don’t think it’s appropriate for anybody to be tracking that. So, I will echo that to anybody involved with the DOJ. And I’m sure it was an oversight. That’s my guess, okay?” he told reporters. 

But lawmakers are demanding answers about why DOJ was tracking their moves in the system, let alone summarizing it in preparation for a hearing.

Rep. Jamie Raskin (D-Md.), the top Democrat on the House Judiciary Committee, said he would be asking DOJ’s Office of Inspector General for an investigation into “this outrageous abuse of power.”

“Bondi and her team are spying on Members of Congress conducting oversight in yet another blatant attempt to intrude into Congress’s oversight processes,” he said in a statement.

Even before the photo, members had inklings their search histories were being monitored by DOJ, which on Monday began allowing lawmakers to review unredacted versions of the files.

“They have a software that we use to view the files, yeah, but they log you into the software, and each member of Congress has a unique username and password, and then every time I log in, I see my search history, which leads me to believe they had access to my search history, and thus could easily track what I was searching,” said Rep. Subhas Subramanyam (D-Va.), who went to review the files earlier this week.

“It only confirmed my suspicion when that picture came out.”

Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.) said she also saw her activity being tracked as she went to view the documents.

“It is disturbing, and it makes you question why they’re doing it. I mean, we know why they’re doing it, right? It’s a form of intimidation, potentially. But, I mean, that’s not going to stop me from going back and reviewing more,” she told The Hill.

She was particularly alarmed to see that information make its way into Bondi’s binder.

“DOJ’s gonna have a really thick binder with all the files I opened up,” she added.

A DOJ spokesperson told the Hill that “DOJ has extended Congress the opportunity to review unredacted documents in the Epstein files. As a part of that review, DOJ logs all searches made on its systems to protect against the release of victim information.”

But Democrats view interest in their searches as a way to both be ready for the attack in yesterday’s hearing and as a way to gain insight into the parties’ oversight of the department.

“Primarily, this was trying to, trying to find out and maybe prebut whatever members were interested in to have some kind of excuse or some kind of comeback for why the Department of Justice hasn’t done its job to investigate or prosecute the folks who are clearly implicated by the files,” Rep. Mary Gay Scanlon (D-Penn.), a member of the panel, told The Hill.

“So we do have separation of powers. Congress’s big job is to conduct oversight…So if she is spying on members of Congress to try to get some kind of advantage in that hearing it just – it stinks.”

Subramanyam also saw it as a way to gain insight into how Democrats may use the files.

“I think they would only use it against us, especially those who are very aggressive in our pursuit of the truth,” he said.

“They might look at search terms that we use and say that, ‘Oh, members of Congress on the Democratic side are investigating Trump’s specific involvement or Melania’s specific involvement.’ There could be a number of ways to use it against us.”

Scanlon said inclusion of the searches in Bondi’s binder was also indicative of her intentions, with the attorney general repeatedly referencing the guide to push back on members, sparking groans from several. 

“She had her, what was it called, the burn book, like out of ‘Mean Girls,’” Scanlon said, referencing the 2004 film.

“She had tabs for each member with, sometimes it was political opposition research, sometimes it was just, ‘Oh, this crime was committed in your community, and you don’t care about it because you’re asking me about something else.’”

Rep. Chip Roy (R-Texas) said the move doesn’t necessarily have to be nefarious but expressed irritation with the department for releasing the files with unnecessary redactions.

“I don’t know what they’re doing looking at what we’re searching for, but I have no concerns. They can know what I’m searching for. I don’t give a crap. I mean, I just want to see the truth,” Roy, a member of the panel, told The Hill.

“If it’s good faith, it would be to be able to answer inquiries and say, ‘Look, this is the issues of areas of concern.’ But you know, like, the biggest thing here is just, let’s just stop all the games. Let’s just get it all out.”

But Democrats did not view it as a good faith effort, and, feeling buoyed about their chances in the midterms, said Bondi further damaged her credibility with a party hoping to take the majority.

“They’re stupid. It’s creepy, and given that there’s some chance we’ll be in the majority, it’s idiotic,” said Rep. Jim Himes (D-Conn.), the top Democrat on the House Intelligence Committee.

“To pass budgets, to arrive at good policy, there needs to be some semblance of partnership, and I guess she blew that up yesterday, not to mention her demeanor. Her demeanor means that she shouldn’t be mayor of a small town in Oklahoma, much less Attorney General.” 

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) called Bondi “petty” and said he wasn’t surprised they were tracking member searches.

“There is no bottom for the Trump administration, for Pam Bondi, for the other sycophants who are part of this corrupt administration, and for Republicans at this point in time. It’s a disgrace. It does violate the principles of separate and coequal branches of government.”

While the House Judiciary Committee has not yet committed to investigating the matter, the top Democrat on the House Oversight Committee said he plans to review it.

“We already knew this Department of Justice was incredibly corrupt, but now Pam Bondi has taken it even further by spying on Members of Congress as they go through the Epstein files. It’s clear we can’t trust anything the DOJ is doing, including their redactions and the withholding of millions of documents. Why are they spying on Members of Congress?” ranking member Robert Garcia (D-Calif.) said in a statement. 

“Pam Bondi is a disgrace, and Oversight Democrats will conduct a full investigation. She needs to resign immediately.”

Emily Brooks and Sudiksha Kochi contributed.

data.title: Russia dismisses Trump tariff threats on Cuba: 'We don't have much trade'
data.description:
The Kremlin on Thursday said it is discussing options with Cuba to provide assistance to the island in the face of a President Trump threatening tariffs on any country providing oil to Havana. 

Kremlin Spokesperson Dmitry Peskov said it is “impossible to discuss these issues in such a public manner,” according to Russian state news outlet RIA Novosti.

Peskov suggested that Russia could be insulated from Trump’s tariff threats since economic ties between the U.S. and Russia were largely severed over Moscow’s 2022 full-scale invasion of Ukraine. 

“We wouldn’t want any escalation, but on the other hand, we don’t have much trade right now,” Peskov said. 

Trump’s attempt to cut off oil exports to has exacerbated an economic crisis and raised dire warnings of a coming humanitarian crisis. Trump levied the threats against Cuba after launching a military operation capturing Venezuela’s President Nicolas Maduro in January and asserting control of the country’s oil exports. Trump suggested that Havana’s leaders, dependent on subsidized Venezuelan oil, would fall next. 

Russia began evacuating its citizens from Cuba but has been forced to adjust its flight schedules because of the fuel shortage. 

But Russia’s Ambassador to Cuba Viktor Koronelli said that embassy evacuations are not on the agenda and that Moscow “will not abandon Cuba.” 

“In light of the extremely difficult energy situation in Cuba, they are counting on help, on a friendly hand from brotherly peoples. And among them, of course, Russia stands in first place,” he said.

The fuel crisis has pushed up food prices for Cubans, led to severe fuel shortages and major power cuts throughout the country, the United Nations said last week. 

The State Department last week said it was delivering $6 million in humanitarian support through two Christian charities “in direct assistance for the Cuban people.”

“We remain vigilant in tracking any diversion or frustration of U.S. assistance efforts, and the regime will be accountable to the United States and its own people for any interference,” it said at the time.

The U.S. embargo has triggered some backlash over humanitarian concerns, with Mexico’s President Claudia Sheinbaum criticising the U.S. for acting “in a very unfair manner.”

“No one can ignore the situation that the Cuban people are currently experiencing because of the sanctions that the United States is imposing in a very unfair manner,” she said during a news conference on Monday, according to the New York Times. 

On Thursday, two Mexican-flagged ships entered Havana harbor loaded with humanitarian aid, Reuters reported. 

Also on Thursday, Chilean Foreign Affairs Minister Alberto van Klaveren said the country will provide monetary assistance for Cuba through multilateral organizations. 

“We all know that there is a very complex situation in Cuba, that there is a population suffering from hunger and that there are also constant power outages,” van Klaveren said, according to Bloomberg.

data.title: Another grand jury fails to indict Trump's political enemies — move on!
data.description:
A federal grand jury this week refused to indict six congressional Democrats over a video where they told service members to reject “illegal orders.”  

Remember that? This is a big deal, not just politically, but legally.  

Sources familiar with the case tell Axios that the Justice Department sought to charge the lawmakers under a statute that carries up to 10 years in prison for anyone who “advises, counsels, urges” insubordination in the military. In the 90-second video posted in November, the six congressional leaders reminded members of the military and intelligence community that “no one has to carry out orders that violate the law or our Constitution.” They did not reference a specific directive.  

After the video was posted, the president called their comments “seditious” and demanded they be “arrested and put on trial.” But now a grand jury declined to indict.  

That outcome fits a broader pattern. Trump’s retribution campaign is hitting a losing streak.  

Federal grand juries almost always return indictments. In 2013, they rejected them just five times out of more than 165,000 cases nationwide, according to the Bureau of Justice Statistics. Prosecutors need only 12 of 23 jurors to agree. The bar is not especially high.  

And yet, in several high-profile cases involving the president’s perceived political adversaries, that bar has not been cleared — or the cases have fallen apart in court.  

For example, charges against former FBI Director James Comey were partially rejected by a grand jury and ultimately dropped after a federal judge ruled that the appointment of hand-picked prosecutor Lindsey Halligan was invalid. In a separate case against New York Attorney General Letitia James, a judge wrote that “all actions flowing from” Halligan’s “defective appointment” are “unlawful exercises of executive power and must be set aside.” A grand jury later declined to indict James a second time.  

Even outside the political arena, juries have pushed back. In November, a former DOJ employee accused of throwing “a sub-style sandwich” at a federal agent was found not guilty. That’s after Pam Bondi pushed to charge him with a felony.  

It’s important to be clear: a grand jury’s refusal to indict is not a declaration of innocence. It simply means prosecutors did not present enough evidence to establish probable cause. But when grand juries — which historically approve the overwhelming majority of federal charges — repeatedly decline to move forward in politically charged cases, that should signal something for the administration.   

The justice system is designed to be slow, procedural and insulated from political pressure. Threats and social media posts don’t substitute for statutory elements or evidentiary standards. Intent must be proven. Appointments must be lawful. Charges must survive judicial scrutiny.  

So while rhetoric may escalate, the courts and juries so far are drawing lines. Maybe it’s time for the administration to take note and set their sights on something other than locking up its perceived political enemies. 

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.

data.title: Thomas Massie slams Pam Bondi, says Epstein files still being hidden
data.description:
Rep. Thomas Massie is demanding answers from Attorney General Pam Bondi and others whom he says are still hiding the truth of the Epstein files from the public. In an interview with Zach Weissmueller, my colleague at Reason magazine, Massie explained why he is willing to have this fight, even if it costs him his job in Congress. Take a look. 

Massie is one of the most libertarian-leaning members of Congress, and also the foremost advocate, along with Rep. Ro Khanna, of releasing the Epstein files. He has regularly made appearances alongside purported survivors of Epstein and demanded the Department of Justice provide information on sexual abusers who participated in Epstein’s crimes. Massie and Khanna accused the feds of failing to properly disclose documents relating to Epstein, a violation of the law, either due to incompetence or maliciousness. 

It seems clear that, unlike so many other political actors who have taken up the cause of Epstein disclosure for self-serving partisan reasons — namely, a quest to disparage the reputations of the Clintons and/or Donald Trump, depending on one’s own partisan valence — Massie and Khanna are engaged in a sincere effort to arrive at the truth. Their complaints that DOJ has mishandled Epstein transparency are well-taken. Bondi previously claimed that she had the Epstein client list “sitting on her desk”; in Epstein parlance, this was taken to mean that there was a list of elite political figures, academics and businessmen for whom Epstein procured underage girls. But no such document has ever been released, and FBI Director Kash Patel subsequently stated that there is no one else involved in Epstein’s sex crimes. 

Most people demanding more answers about Epstein have alleged a vast and sinister conspiracy, involving not just Epstein but other powerful abusers. At yesterday’s House Judiciary hearing, lawmakers accused Bondi of failing not just Epstein’s victims, but hundreds of young women victimized by Epstein and his associates. 

If there are other people who sexually abused underage girls, the public ought to know about it. But that remains an open question, since none of the files substantiate the idea that Epstein procured underage girls for a cabal of sexual abusers. 

The files do, however, reveal the names of people who met with Epstein, took money from Epstein, or continued to email with Epstein over the years. These people are not accused of crimes; in many cases, the idea that the named individual did something particularly bad seems to spring from a misconception.  

In other words, releasing the files has resulted in some real world harms. 

One of those folks is Mark Tramo. Tramo is an associate adjunct professor of neurology at the University of California, Los Angeles. Whether he will remain in that position is unknown. A sign-wielding woman has reportedly camped herself outside the building where he works, and has demanded that UCLA fire him. 

What was Tramo’s crime? Nothing. No one has accused him of any crime whatsoever. His moral failing, according to his critics, is that he emailed Epstein with some frequency over the years, including about his scientific research and areas of academic interest. 

In one such email, he mentioned reading a study that found “a newborn will suck on a pacifier more vigorously” if the baby hears his own mother’s voice rather than some other woman’s. 

Judging by the reaction on X, social media users have found this email highly incriminating. It’s what the woman with the sign referenced when she screamed at the department that Tramo should lose his job. 

The conservative writer Rod Dreher is with her, writing: “God bless this woman! What kind of monster must Mark Tramo be?” 

People seem to have imagined that Tramo was giving Epstein advice about something very graphic. But this notion is patently absurd. 

Tramo was a professor of neurology. 

He had co-authored articles on the effects of music on premature babies. He had received funding from Epstein to create a research institution for the study of music and the brain. The idea that he was assisting Epstein in the sexual abuse of babies — instead of just sharing an interesting fact related to his area of academic expertise — is extremely far-fetched.  

Frankly, the idea that Epstein would have been interested in this knowledge for disgusting sexual reasons is also quite doubtful. Epstein was convicted of sexual abusing teenage girls; there is no evidence he was sexually attracted to babies. 

Nevertheless, since Tramo’s communications with Epstein continued after the disgraced financier’s 2008 incarceration for sexual misconduct, he is among the names of people considered to have associated with a known abuser. In statements to the media, Tramo denied having knowledge of Epstein’s specific crimes, saying that he thought Epstein had been convicted of prostitution. 

He said: “I myself had not heard anything [about] statutory rape or minors being involved, I never saw him with young girls, never visited the island, never flew in his plane. I was introduced to him by the Harvard Provost—he donated several millions to Harvard and was dangling a lot more.” 

It is unclear if UCLA is taking any action against Tramo, though the university website did remove his profile page on its media list of designated field experts. UCLA did not immediately respond to a request for comment. 

In the meantime, Tramo told me via email that he has been besieged with hate mail, including death threats. 

Now Tramo, for what it’s worth, asserts that he did not have specific knowledge about the nature of Epstein’s crimes, the details of which were mostly unknown to the public until a 2018 expose in The Miami-Herald. 

Massie takes the view, however that anyone who associated with Epstein after 2018 is fair game.

Is that fair? You be the judge. 

Robby Soave is co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising” and a senior editor for Reason Magazine. This column is an edited transcription of his daily commentary.
